common_name: Tasti-Lee Tomato (University of Florida High-Lycopene Red Slicing Tomato with Superior Flavor)

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting; Tasti-Lee is an indeterminate tomato released by the University of Florida IFAS program with both disease resistance appropriate for Gulf South conditions and substantially higher lycopene content than standard red tomato varieties. In zone 9, transplant March–April. The elevated lycopene content produces a slightly more orange-red skin color than typical red tomatoes — the skin appears almost orange-red rather than crimson red. Tasti-Lee's disease resistance package (Fusarium, Verticillium, root-knot nematodes) addresses the primary limitations of home tomato production in zone 9. The variety was released with the specific goal of delivering both health value (high lycopene) and genuine flavor — a combination not typical of the commercial tomato market, which has historically prioritized yield, appearance, and shelf life over flavor and nutrition.

directions: Start seeds indoors; transplant after last frost and stake or cage. Harvest when uniformly red-orange and gives to slight pressure. Tasti-Lee Tomato was released by the University of Florida Institute of Food and Agricultural Sciences (UF/IFAS) around 2010, developed through a breeding program that specifically targeted the combination of high lycopene content, improved flavor, and Gulf South disease resistance in a single red slicing tomato variety. The UF/IFAS vegetable breeding program, one of the most active in the southeastern United States, has produced multiple important commercial tomato varieties for Florida's significant commercial tomato industry; Tasti-Lee represents a branch of that program targeting quality attributes (flavor, nutrition) rather than the shipping durability and uniformity that commercial Florida tomato breeding has primarily emphasized. The variety's elevated lycopene content — documented by UF/IFAS researchers as substantially higher than industry-standard slicing tomatoes — combined with higher-than-typical sugar content and genuine tomato flavor creates a profile closer to the flavor of a well-grown heirloom tomato than most disease-resistant hybrid varieties provide. The variety has been noted by home gardeners and researchers for producing genuinely good-flavored tomatoes in zone 9's challenging summer heat, a combination difficult to achieve with either heirloom varieties (which lack disease resistance) or standard commercial varieties (which sacrifice flavor for durability). Tasti-Lee is sold through specialty seed companies and has been grown for research comparisons confirming its superior flavor and nutritional profile relative to commercial standards.
sources: University of Florida IFAS — Tasti-Lee tomato release and high-lycopene breeding documentation
UF/IFAS Extension — Flavor and Nutrition-Focused Tomato Variety Development
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Tomato Variety Evaluation for Gulf South Home Gardens

common_name: Sunburst Squash (AAS-Winning Golden Yellow Pattypan, Compact and Prolific Summer Squash)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Sunburst is a bush-type pattypan/scallop squash with a compact growth habit that is significantly more manageable in small gardens than the sprawling zucchini types. In zone 9, plant March–May and August–September for fall production. The distinctive golden-yellow color at harvest maturity is an important cue: harvest Sunburst when the fruits are 2–3 inches across and uniformly golden-yellow. At this small size the skin is tender and edible, the flesh is firm and slightly sweet, and the distinctive scalloped edge with pale yellow skin is at its most attractive for presentation. Larger fruits (5+ inches) become tough-skinned and starchy. Sunburst's compact plant habit and prolific production make it one of the best summer squash varieties for containers and raised beds in zone 9.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost; thin to 24–36 inches. Harvest at 2–3 inches when golden-yellow and edges still tender. Sunburst Squash is an All-America Selections (AAS) winning summer squash recognized for its distinctive golden-yellow color, compact plant habit, and excellent productivity in home garden conditions. The pattypan or scallop squash form (a flattened disc with scalloped edges, sometimes called 'flying saucer' for its distinctive shape) represents an older squash form that predates the elongated zucchini in American kitchen garden history; pattypan varieties were among the first summer squash grown widely in early American gardens, and the distinctive scalloped shape remains one of the most visually attractive squash forms. Sunburst's breakthrough was producing the pattypan form in a vivid, uniform golden-yellow rather than the pale yellow, white, or green of earlier pattypan varieties — the bright color made it immediately eye-catching at farmers markets and in garden displays, and its AAS recognition reflected consistent performance across trial locations. The flavor of small Sunburst squash is mild and slightly sweet, with a firmer texture than zucchini at comparable sizes — the denser flesh of scallop types holds up better in sauté, roasting, and stuffed preparations than the waterier flesh of standard zucchini. For zone 9 gardeners, the compact plant size and abundant production of Sunburst make it one of the more practical summer squash choices for space-limited raised beds and containers, where a standard zucchini plant's 3–4 foot spread would be excessive.
sources: All-America Selections — Sunburst squash AAS recognition and trial performance documentation
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Sunburst pattypan squash variety notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Summer Squash Variety Selection for the Gulf South

common_name: Peter Pan Squash (AAS-Winning Pale Green Scallop Squash, Early and Compact)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Peter Pan is a scallop (pattypan) squash that produces pale sage-green to silvery-green flat, scallop-edged fruits on a compact bush plant. In zone 9, plant March–May and August–September for fall production. Harvest Peter Pan at 3–4 inches when the skin is still tender and the green color is uniform and pale — the skin toughens significantly if the squash is left to grow beyond 4–5 inches. The distinctive pale green-grey color (lighter than most green summer squash) and scalloped edge make Peter Pan one of the most immediately recognizable summer squash varieties at harvest. The flavor is mild and nutty, somewhat different from zucchini in a pleasant way — scallop types have a slightly different flesh texture and a subtly nuttier quality that distinguishes them from elongated zucchini types.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost; thin to 24–36 inches. Harvest at 3–4 inches when pale sage-green and tender. Peter Pan Squash is an All-America Selections (AAS) winner in the summer squash category, honored for its early production, compact plant habit, and the distinctive pale sage-green to silvery-blue-green color of the scallop-shaped fruits. The AAS recognition confirmed Peter Pan's performance advantage over other scallop squash varieties of its time in trials across multiple growing regions and climates. The scallop squash form — a flat, disc-shaped fruit with distinctively scalloped or fluted edges — is one of the oldest American summer squash forms, with historical precedent in both Native American cultivation and early colonial American gardens. Peter Pan's F1 hybrid vigor contributes to both its compactness (more manageable plant than many bush zucchini) and productivity (abundant fruit set throughout the season). The color is a notable characteristic: the pale, muted sage-green to silvery green of Peter Pan is unlike the darker green of standard zucchini or the yellow of Sunburst, occupying its own distinctive color space that many gardeners find attractive for mixed variety plantings and market displays where color diversity is valued. The flat scallop form also makes Peter Pan an exceptionally attractive stuffed squash: the naturally hollow interior of the scallop form (relative to the solid flesh core of zucchini) allows more filling capacity per fruit when halved for stuffing, and the shallow depth of the squash makes even cooking possible at the small sizes most suitable for stuffing preparations.
sources: All-America Selections — Peter Pan scallop squash AAS recognition documentation
Burpee Seeds — Peter Pan squash compact summer squash variety notes
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Summer Squash Variety Evaluation for Home Gardens

common_name: Eight Ball Squash (Round Dark Green Zucchini, Compact Summer Squash for Stuffing)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Eight Ball is a round zucchini type producing perfectly round, dark green fruits on a compact, upright bush plant. In zone 9, plant March–May and again August–September for fall production. The round form is Eight Ball's defining characteristic — rather than the elongated cylinder of standard zucchini, Eight Ball fruits are globose (round), producing a shape ideal for hollowing and stuffing. Harvest at 3–4 inches diameter (roughly golf ball to baseball size) for best flavor and tenderness; the skin toughens and the flesh becomes starchy if allowed to grow to tennis ball or softball size. Eight Ball's compact plant (18–24 inches tall) makes it excellent for raised beds and containers. The round shape also means the squash sits stably when stuffed and baked, without rolling — a practical advantage over elongated zucchini in stuffed preparations.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost; thin to 18–24 inches. Harvest at 3–4 inches diameter when skin is dark green and glossy. Eight Ball is a compact, round-fruited zucchini variety that produces unusually shaped globose fruits — perfectly spherical when harvested at optimal size — on upright, compact bush plants significantly smaller than standard elongated zucchini types. The name 'Eight Ball' references the eight-ball billiards ball — a solid black sphere — which the dark green, round squash resembles at small harvest size. The round form is the primary distinctive quality: while standard zucchini is selected for elongated cylindrical shape that makes it easy to slice into uniform rounds, Eight Ball's round form creates a naturally shaped container ideal for hollowing and stuffing. For stuffed squash preparations, Eight Ball provides several practical advantages: the round form creates a stable base for baking without additional support, the natural cavity when the top is removed is more generous relative to fruit size than elongated squash, and individual fruits at optimal 3–4 inch harvest size provide a single-serving portion that is visually appealing when presented whole. The flavor is typical mild zucchini with slightly denser flesh than elongated types at comparable harvest sizes — the round form concentrates more flesh relative to surface area than a cylinder of comparable diameter. Eight Ball was developed and released by Park Seed, with the AAS award confirming performance across diverse North American trial conditions.
sources: Park Seed — Eight Ball round zucchini variety development and AAS recognition
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Round and compact summer squash variety comparison
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Summer Squash Production and Variety Selection

common_name: Long Island Improved Dill (Classic Standard Dill Variety for Leaf and Seed Harvest)

planting.note: Direct sow only — dill does not transplant well; plant in early spring several weeks before last frost or in fall in zone 9. Long Island Improved is one of the classic American dill varieties, selected as a standard cultivar for both dill weed (leaf) production and dill seed harvest. In zone 9, plant October–November for fall-winter production (the best season) and February–March for a short spring crop that bolts with summer heat. Unlike Dukat dill (selected for leaf oil content), Long Island Improved is a dual-purpose variety with good leaf production and large, well-formed seed heads appropriate for dill seed harvest and pickling. The plants grow tall (3–4 feet) with the characteristic blue-green ferny foliage, and produce flat-topped flower umbels that dry to attractive seed heads.

directions: Direct sow in cool soil; thin to 6–12 inches. For seed harvest, allow plants to flower and seed heads to mature and begin to brown. Long Island Improved Dill is a classic American named dill variety that has been one of the standard cultivars in the United States seed trade for many decades — one of the most widely available and commonly grown dill varieties in home and market garden production. The 'Long Island' designation reflects its association with New York's Long Island, which was a major center of market garden and specialty crop production in the 19th and early 20th centuries, with many vegetable varieties named for the region (Long Island Cheese Pumpkin, Long Island Improved Brussels Sprouts). Unlike the more recent specialty dill cultivars selected for specific properties (Dukat for high leaf oil, Fernleaf for dwarf compact habit), Long Island Improved is a classic standard selection valued for its combination of good leaf production and excellent seed head development — the characteristic that makes it a true dual-purpose variety useful for both dill weed (fresh and dried leaf) and dill seed harvest. The seed heads of Long Island Improved are large, flat-topped umbels that dry well for use in pickling (dill seed is the primary flavoring agent in dill pickles), in dill vinegar, and as a dried floral/culinary ingredient. The seeds are held firmly in the umbel until fully ripe and begin to shatter as the seed head fully dries — harvest by cutting the entire seed head when seeds have changed from green to tan-brown but before significant shattering begins. For zone 9 gardeners wanting dill seeds for pickling their summer cucumbers, Long Island Improved's good seed head development makes it the classic choice.
sources: W. Atlee Burpee — Long Island Improved dill variety historical documentation
Seed Savers Exchange — Standard American dill variety documentation
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Dill Production in the Gulf South
